serving.natural_language_portion: One standard side dish portion of celery with bean curd sticks is approximately 200 g, typically served as a shared vegetable side at a Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Celery, raw (FDC ID 169988) and Tofu, dried-frozen (koyadofu) (FDC ID 172443); Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient composition data used for dish-level sodium and seasoning adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the composite dish 'Celery with Bean Curd Sticks'. Nutrient values were derived by adapting USDA FoodData Central data for raw celery (FDC ID 169988) and dried bean curd sticks / yuba (FDC ID 172443) in approximate proportions (60% celery, 35% rehydrated bean curd sticks, 5% seasoning), with sodium adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce addition consistent with Singapore HPB Foodo dish-level data for comparable Chinese vegetable side dishes. Input record values (energy 90 kcal, protein 6 g, fat 5 g, carbohydrate 6 g) are consistent with this component-based estimate and have been retained. Cholesterol confirmed 0 mg as both celery and bean curd sticks are plant-derived foods.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Tofu (soybean curd) GI ~15; celery GI negligible.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite dish 'Celery with Bean Curd Sticks'. GI estimated at 15 based on the dominant soy-protein component (bean curd sticks / yuba), using the published GI of tofu/soybean curd (~15) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Celery contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially affect GI. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (15 × 12.0) / 100 = 1.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 325 mg per 100 g from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 325 mg/100 g (650 mg per standard 200 g serving) is borderline for hypertension management; patients should request reduced-sodium soy sauce and limit portion to 100–150 g to keep sodium intake within a single-dish target of under 400 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6 g/100 g), low GI (~15), high plant protein (6 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 15 and only 6 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish produces a glycemic load of 1.8 per 200 g serving, making it an excellent low-glycemic side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; the plant protein from bean curd sticks further moderates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.75 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, plant protein, and celery polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat (0.75 g/100 g) and contains no cholesterol, with plant-based protein from bean curd sticks and anti-inflammatory polyphenols (apigenin, luteolin) from celery; the primary cardiovascular caution is the sodium content from soy sauce, which should be minimised by using reduced-sodium soy sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols from celery; low saturated fat; calcium 75 mg/100 g from bean curd sticks
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Celery provides apigenin and luteolin with documented anti-inflammatory properties, and bean curd sticks supply calcium (75 mg/100 g) supporting bone health in rheumatoid arthritis; sodium from soy sauce should be minimised to reduce risk of fluid retention and joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Celery with Bean Curd Sticks (芹菜腐竹, Qín cài fǔ zhú) is a classic Chinese vegetable side dish widely served in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. It combines crisp, fibrous celery stalks with rehydrated bean curd sticks (yuba or tofu skin — the dried film formed on heated soy milk), seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, and garlic. The dish may be prepared by stir-frying over high heat for a warm, lightly charred version, or by blanching and cold-tossing for a refreshing chilled salad. The texture contrast between crunchy celery and the chewy, slightly spongy bean curd sticks is a defining characteristic. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 90 kcal per 100 g, with 6 g plant protein from the bean curd sticks, 5 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from sesame oil), and only 6 g carbohydrate, yielding a very low glycemic index of approximately 15. Dietary fibre at 2 g/100 g and calcium at 75 mg/100 g are notable micronutrient contributions. Sodium at 325 mg/100 g reflects soy sauce seasoning and is the primary clinical consideration for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is fully plant-based (vegan and vegetarian), soy-allergen-bearing, and suitable for most dietary patterns. It is commonly served as a shared side dish at Chinese family meals, hawker centres, and zi char restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the composite dish 'Celery with Bean Curd Sticks' in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, or other approved databases. Nutrient values were derived by component-based adaptation from USDA FoodData Central entries for celery and dried bean curd sticks, with sodium adjusted for soy sauce seasoning consistent with HPB Foodo comparable dish data. The input record values are consistent with this estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on published tofu GI data. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; the sesame oil component contributes predominantly MUFA and PUFA but exact per-100g dish-level values cannot be reliably disaggregated without a full recipe analysis. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite dish is not reported in the approved sources consulted; celery and bean curd sticks both contribute potassium but dish-level values are not available. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this specific composite dish in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — both celery and bean curd sticks are plant-derived foods containing no industrially produced trans fats; sesame oil does not contribute trans fats. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — all ingredients (celery, bean curd sticks, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic) are plant-derived; cholesterol is naturally zero. 6. Ethnic tags 'Drinks & Beverages' and 'Noodles' present in the input record's cultural.ethnic_tags field have been removed as they are clearly erroneous for this dish. 7. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on published tofu/soybean curd GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources.
